ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Apr-09-2019 Continued To Jun-11-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-19-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jessica C. Shafer, Esq., O'Connor & Associates, 415-693-9960, appearing for Plaintiff;
Derek F. Horan, Esq., Morrison & foerster, 415-268-6323, appearing for Defendant(s) Enda Quigley et al;

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ENDA QUIGLEY, SEAN MURPHY, DANIEL WALSH DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT

Defendants Enda Quigley, Sean Murphy and Daniel Walsh's demurrer to plaintiff Christopher Flood's complaint is SUSTAINED as to both causes of action without leave to amend.

As to the fiduciary breach claim, defendants owed no fiduciary duty to plaintiff as a matter of law. As pled, Buyer LLC was a manager-managed limited liability company, and members of such an LLC owe one another no fiduciary duty by reason of being members. (Cmplt. Exh. A 5.1; Corp. Code 17704.09(f)(3).) Only its manager owes duties of loyalty and care to a member of a manager-managed LLC. (Id. at (f)(1); Feresi v. The Livery, LLC (2014) 232 Cal.App.4th 419, 425.)

Plaintiff's argument that majority-owner members of an LLC owe fiduciary duties to minority-owner members fails for two reasons, each independently dispositive. First, plaintiff's complaint shows that the demurring defendants were not members of Buyer LLC. (Id. at Ex A 2.16.) Second, as already noted, the plain language of the Revised Uniform Limited Liability Company Act (RULLCA) says no fiduciary duty exists between members of a manager-managed LLC. (Corp. Code 17704.09(f)(3).) Jones v. Ahmanson (1969) 1 Cal.3d 93 reflects common law that pre-dated the RULLCA and does not regard an LLC in any event. Plaintiff cites no case holding that the RULLCA gives minority-owner members rights to sue majority owners for breach of fiduciary duty in a manager-managed LLC. And again, the RULLCA is to the contrary.

The fraudulent concealment claim also fails. For the reasons set out above, defendants owed plaintiff no fiduciary duty, which means there was no duty to disclose. (Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum Commission v. Insomniac, Inc. (2015) 233 Cal.App.4th 803, 831.)

While many of them appear well-made, defendants' other points need not be reached.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Giselle Casey, CSR#8098 email: gcasey@sftc.org, Reported. =(302/RBU)
